channel.title: MiSTerZine
channel.description: Short, source-checked tidbits on MiSTer FPGA releases, written by AI from cited sources and verified before publishing.

item.title: Breakout
item.description:
Before they started Apple, Steve Jobs took a job from game maker Atari to build Breakout cheap, then handed the real work to his friend Steve Wozniak. Woz packed it onto just 44 parts, so dense that, by his own account, Atari's engineers couldn't follow how it worked. The factory gave up and rebuilt it their way with about 100. Same game, twice the parts.

50th anniversary; MiSTer debut 2020-12-12. Sources: Wikipedia.

item.title: Night Slashers
item.description:
Fighting through zombies, vampires, and werewolves wasn't the only thing locked down in Night Slashers: game maker Data East scrambled the arcade code itself, hiding it behind an encrypted processor. rmonic79's FPGA core doesn't cheat with pre-cracked copies. It unscrambles the code live as it runs, exactly as the original board did.

MiSTer debut 2026-07-10. Sources: Wikipedia, core repo.

item.title: Trio The Punch - Never Forget Me...
item.description:
Trio The Punch began life as something else entirely: a planned board game in the vein of Western snakes and ladders, scrapped and rebuilt from scratch during development. What game maker Data East shipped instead, Japan-only, was a surreal game across 35 levels where jumping on enemy bullets bounces your character off like a trampoline, with the laws of physics tossed out.

MiSTer debut 2026-07-08. Sources: Wikipedia, core repo.

item.title: Battle Garegga
item.description:
In the arcade shooter Battle Garegga, thirty years old this February, powering up your weapon and grabbing items pushes the difficulty higher, while dying drops it. The catch: the fewer lives you hold when you die, the more the difficulty falls. Players are forced to keep themselves powered down, conserve shots, and lose lives on purpose to keep the final stages playable.

30th anniversary; MiSTer debut 2023-09-10. Sources: Wikipedia.

item.title: Cabal
item.description:
Cabal, a 1988 arcade game from developer TAD Corporation, placed your character in the foreground and watched from behind the shoulder, the way modern third-person shooters do. It was successful enough to spawn a wave of imitators in the years after, among them NAM-1975 and Wild Guns.

MiSTer debut 2026-07-05. Sources: Wikipedia.

item.title: Biomechanical Toy
item.description:
A criminal named Scrubby breaks out of jail, locked up for trying to steal the pendulum that brings toys to life. That's the hook of game maker Gaelco's 1995 run-and-gun Biomechanical Toy, where a hero named Inguz shoots his way through evil toyland. Its first console appearance came on an Evercade compilation.

MiSTer debut 2026-07-05. Sources: Wikipedia.

item.title: Flash Boy
item.description:
Imagine dropping a coin into an arcade cabinet, then waiting two to three minutes before the game even starts. That was the deal with Flash Boy, a 1981 release from game maker Data East for the DECO Cassette System, where each game arrived on a standard microcassette tape. Slot one in and the cabinet copied the whole program into its memory before you could play.

MiSTer debut 2026-06-30. Sources: Wikipedia.

item.title: Darius II (Dual Screen)
item.description:
Darius II was built to sprawl. Following the original Darius, game maker Taito programmed it to stretch across several screens at once. A three-screen version exists, but the two-screen layout became the usual way to play it, which is what this core runs. Outside Japan it went by a different name: Sagaia.

MiSTer debut 2026-06-16. Sources: Wikipedia.
